title: HQTN-SER: Speech Emotion Recognition with Hybrid Quantum Tensor Networks
authors: Mahad Mohtashim, Nouhaila Innan, Muhammad Shafique
abstract: Speech emotion recognition (SER) remains fragile in real-world conditions because emotional cues are subtle, speaker-dependent, and easily confounded by recording variability, while high-performing deep models typically rely on large and carefully curated training sets. Quantum machine learning offers an alternative way to introduce nonlinear correlation modeling with compact modules, yet existing quantum SER studies remain limited and the impact of circuit structure is not well understood. This paper presents HQTN-SER, a hybrid quantum-classical framework that investigates how quantum tensor network connectivity can support SER under small-qubit settings. HQTN-SER introduces (i) an MPS-inspired quantum tensor network module that enforces structured interactions to model correlations in speech representations with a small number of trainable parameters, and (ii) a fusion strategy that combines quantum measurement features with a learned classical latent embedding for end-to-end emotion classification. We evaluate HQTN-SER on three public benchmarks (RAVDESS, SAVEE, and MDER) under a unified preprocessing and training protocol. The proposed model achieves consistent performance across datasets, RAVDESS = 80.12%, SAVEE = 78.26% and MDER = 73.51% accuracy, with stable convergence and low qubit counts, showing that tensor network structure can be an effective and hardware-aware design choice for quantum-assisted SER. The results provide a reproducible baseline and clarify when structured quantum modules can add value to affective computing today.
summary: This paper introduces a hybrid quantum-classical framework for speech emotion recognition that uses a Matrix Product State (MPS) inspired quantum circuit. By using a structured tensor network approach, the model efficiently captures correlations in acoustic features using very few qubits and remains robust to simulated hardware noise. It provides a scalable, hardware-aware design for applying quantum machine learning to complex signal processing tasks.
main_problem: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) is fragile in real-world conditions due to subtle cues and high parameter requirements of classical models, while existing quantum machine learning approaches lack the architectural inductive bias needed to model correlated acoustic features.
main_result: The HQTN-SER framework achieves high accuracy (up to 80.12% on RAVDESS) and demonstrates significant robustness to hardware noise, showing that MPS-inspired quantum circuits can effectively model speech correlations with very low qubit counts.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical pipeline using PCA for dimensionality reduction, an MPS-inspired variational quantum circuit for feature extraction, and a fusion strategy that combines quantum measurement statistics with classical latent embeddings.
model_or_system: A hybrid quantum-classical model utilizing a 1D chain of 3 to 4 qubits with an MPS-style architecture featuring angle encoding (Ry rotations), parameterized Ry and Rz gates, and nearest-neighbor CNOT entanglement.
key_observables: Expectation values of single-qubit Z operators.
important_parameters: Small qubit counts (n=3, 4), shallow circuit depth, and 32-dimensional PCA-compressed input features.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the structured connectivity of a tensor network can act as a sufficient inductive bias for speech features and does not claim unconditional quantum advantage.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the HQTN-SER workflow; Figure 2 details the MPS-structured variational circuit; Figures 3-5 display training/validation loss and accuracy curves across datasets.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of SER fragility, proposes the HQTN-SER framework, details the MPS-inspired architecture and fusion strategy, presents experimental results on three datasets, performs ablation and hardware-noise studies, and concludes with future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of the HQTN-SER workflow for speech emotion recognition. Raw audio (.wav) from SAVEE, RAVDESS, and MDER is converted into 128-dimensional MFCC features (Librosa) and reduced to 32 dimensions using PCA. The resulting feature vector is mapped into a shared representation and processed in parallel by a classical encoder and an MPS-structured variational quantum circuit; their outputs are concatenated and fed to a final classifier to produce the emotion prediction.

Fig. 2: MPS-structured variational circuit used in HQTN-SER. Trainable single-qubit rotations (Ry, Rz) are interleaved with nearest-neighbor CNOT gates in a left-to-right sweep. Measurement statistics are obtained as expectation values on each qubit and used as quantum features.
